mnemonics.title: PAGE FAULT MEMORY
mnemonics.content: PAGE FAULT: Process Accesses - Generate trap - Find frame - Fetch page - Update table - Resume process. THIS HELPS RECALL THE PAGE FAULT HANDLING STEPS.

mnemonics.title: FIFO LRU OPT
mnemonics.content: FIFO: FIRST IN FIRST OUT (OLDEST EVICTED) - LRU: LEAST RECENTLY USED (OLDEST IN TIME) - OPT: OPTIMAL (FUTURE KNOWLEDGE). THIS HELPS DIFFERENTIATE REPLACEMENT ALGORITHMS.

mnemonics.title: SWAPPING MEMORY
mnemonics.content: SWAP IN: Disk to Memory - SWAP OUT: Memory to Disk. S-I-M (Swap In Memory) HELPS RECALL DIRECTION.

acronyms.usage: Hardware cache for virtual-to-physical address translation
Memory optimization technique during fork() system call
Page replacement algorithm based on temporal locality
Page replacement algorithm using arrival time
Memory management technique providing illusion of larger memory
CPU scheduling (not memory, but commonly confused)

visualTips.visualization: Think of a book divided into fixed-size chapters (pages) stored on a shelf (frames). You can read pages in any order by finding where each chapter is physically stored.

Think of a library with different sections: fiction, reference, periodicals. Each section (segment) has variable size and different purposes, with an index (segment table) telling you where each section starts.
Imagine a hotel directory showing room numbers (frames) for each guest name (virtual page). The directory maps names to actual room locations.
Imagine a person constantly taking books off a shelf, reading one page, putting it back, and getting another - spending more time fetching than reading.

analogies.analogy: Like a library's hold system - you don't need to have every book in the library in your personal shelf. When you need a book not on your shelf, you request it and it arrives, while another returns.
Like a small desk with limited space. When you need a new paper (page), you must put back one you're done using. The challenge is choosing which paper you'll need least recently.
Like a frequently-used phone number saved in your contacts instead of looking it up every time. The TLB 'remembers' recent address translations.
Like the open books on your desk - the ones you're actively using right now. The OS keeps these 'open' in fast memory while less-used pages are on the 'bookshelf' (disk).
